turns.text: Hello Kelly, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking, really. It’s just… it’s hard to put it into words because most days I’m still just trying to convince myself that I’m actually home. 

You look at the photos online, the ones of mothers holding their babies in those calm, glowing rooms, and that’s what I expected. But for me, it felt like I was being hunted by my own body. By the second trimester, I wasn’t a person to my doctors; I was just a collection of numbers and genetic markers. They’d look at my chart, frown, and talk *about* me, not *to* me. The pre-eclampsia just sat there like a shadow, waiting. 

When things finally tipped over into the eclampsia… the seizure… it wasn’t like the movies where you wake up and everyone is smiling. It felt like I’d been shredded and put back together wrong. Now, I’m sitting here in Lee, watching the fog, and every time I see a white coat or even a sterile-looking hallway in a show, my heart just hammers against my ribs. I’m only twenty-two, but I feel like I’ve lived an entire lifetime of terror. I’m just trying to figure out how to be a mom without waiting for the next emergency to pull the rug out from under us again.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

Honestly? I thought recovery would be about exhaustion, maybe some stitches, and just learning to navigate those first few weeks of sleepless nights with a newborn. I expected to be tired—everyone tells you that—and I thought I’d be dealing with the typical soreness. I guess I thought I’d be "me" again pretty quickly, just with a little more laundry and a baby to feed.

The reality? It wasn't just physical pain; it was like my body had gone on strike. Because of the eclampsia, the recovery wasn't just about healing a wound; it was about recovering from a medical emergency that almost killed me. I had this constant, buzzing anxiety that I was going to have another seizure at any second. Every time I had a headache, my heart would start pounding, and I’d be staring at the wall, waiting for the lights to flicker again. 

I’m also still struggling with this deep sense of betrayal. My body felt like a traitor. I feel weak in a way that has nothing to do with muscles and everything to do with how fragile I feel living in my own skin now. It’s not just a "healing process"—it’s a constant, daily battle to feel like I’m actually safe in my own house.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I think I had this romanticized idea that feeding would be this quiet, bonding time. You see the pictures of mothers and babies locked in this perfect, instinctual gaze, and I really wanted that. I figured it would be natural—maybe a little tricky at first, but something my body and the baby would just *know* how to do. I didn't worry much about the mechanics of it; I just wanted to feel that connection, especially after feeling so disconnected from my own body during the pregnancy.

The reality was… chaotic. Between the meds they had me on and the sheer exhaustion of the seizure recovery, my brain was so foggy I could barely keep track of the time. When I tried to breastfeed, I felt like I was constantly failing. My milk didn't come in the way the books said it would, and the pressure was immense. Between the nurses checking my blood pressure every hour and the internal panic that I was somehow going to pass that stress or the medication through to my baby, I felt like a disaster. I ended up switching to formula just so I could actually sleep for an hour and stop feeling like I was failing at every single turn. It wasn't the bonding experience I imagined; it was just one more thing that made me feel like I wasn't doing this "motherhood" thing right.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

That’s the thing—the support felt so… cold. It felt like they were checking boxes on a form. The nurses would come in and ask, "Are you breastfeeding?" and when I’d say I was struggling, they’d give me this standard, clinical advice about positioning or latching. They were talking about *mechanics*, like I was a machine that needed to be calibrated. 

What I actually needed was someone to look at me, really look at me, and see that I was terrified. I needed someone to say, "Kelly, you’ve been through a trauma. Your body is recovering from a seizure. It’s okay if things aren't working like they are in the pamphlets." Instead, it felt like more pressure. I felt like if I couldn't get the breastfeeding to work, it was just another sign that my body was broken. 

When I finally decided to use formula, there was this subtle shift in how they treated me—like I’d taken the easy way out, or like I hadn't tried hard enough. Nobody asked me how I *felt* about the change. Nobody acknowledged that I was mourning the experience I thought I was supposed to have. They were all business, and I just needed someone to be a human being with me. It just made me feel even more isolated in that hospital room.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I expected to be the "tired but happy" mom. I knew there would be some hormones, maybe a few weepy days because of the exhaustion, but I honestly thought the hard part was the pregnancy itself. I kept telling myself that once the baby was safely out, the looming shadow of the pre-eclampsia would just lift. I expected to finally be able to breathe, thinking, *“Okay, you made it, you’re safe now.”*

But the reality? The silence of home is almost louder than the hospital was. When I’m here alone in Lee, without the machines and the doctors, my mind just plays the tape of that delivery room over and over. It’s not just "baby blues"—it’s like I’m constantly waiting for the other shoe to drop. I look at my daughter and I get this rush of love, followed immediately by this suffocating, sharp fear that something is going to happen to me and she’ll be left alone. 

Every time I feel a little lightheaded or a bit of a headache, I don't think, *“I need some water,”* I think, *“This is it. It’s happening again.”* It’s a constant, low-grade terror. I feel like a stranger in my own life. I’m physically here, but mentally, I’m still stuck in that delivery room, trying to wake up.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

I think there’s this unspoken rule that you’re supposed to be so grateful, right? Everyone tells you, "At least you’re both here," or "The baby is healthy, that’s all that matters." And, of course, that *is* all that matters. I know that. But it feels like that gratitude is a cage. It’s like I’m not "allowed" to admit that I’m drowning because it might make people think I don't love my child enough or that I’m ungrateful for surviving.

The professionals were the worst for that. They’d see me looking blank or anxious, and they’d just push a pamphlet on postpartum depression into my hand or tell me to "focus on the joy." It felt like they wanted me to fast-forward through the trauma so they could clear me from their list. 

And my own family? They’re supportive, but they treat me like I’m made of glass now. I want to scream that I’m angry—that I’m angry at my own body for failing, angry at the doctors for how they treated me, and angry that I didn't get the birth experience I deserved. But when you’re a twenty-two-year-old mom, people just want to see you smile and coo at the baby. So, I swallow it. I keep the terror and the anger behind my teeth.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before this, I think I saw my future as this linear path. I was Kelly, I had my job, I had my life in Lee, and I was going to be a mother on top of all that. I expected the baby to be an addition to who I already was—an accessory to my identity, in a way. I thought I’d just keep moving forward, just with a little more responsibility.

But now? That version of Kelly feels like a ghost. I’ve been stripped down to nothing but a "patient" and a "mother," and those two things feel like they’re at war with each other. I don't feel like a twenty-two-year-old anymore; I feel like a survivor of something most people only hear about in medical textbooks. 

My identity now is entirely defined by this hyper-vigilance. I’m not just Kelly; I’m the woman who has to monitor her blood pressure like it’s a scoreboard. I look in the mirror and I don't recognize the person looking back. I’m scared that the "me" I was—the one who had plans and wasn't afraid of a hospital—is gone forever. I’m trying to figure out how to be more than just a medical file, but when you spend every day fighting off panic, there’s not much room left for *me*.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

Honestly, the professional part is the one that hurts the most. I worked so hard to get to where I was at work—I took pride in being reliable, in being the person who could get things done. Before the pregnancy, I was building something. Now, even thinking about going back feels impossible. How do I go back to a desk and act like everything is normal when I’m still jumping at shadows? My coworkers, they don’t know what "eclampsia" actually feels like. To them, it’s just a medical leave. To me, it’s the time I died a little bit. 

Socially, I’ve just drifted away. My friends are doing twenty-two-year-old things—going out, talking about dating, worrying about their jobs—and I’m sitting here worrying if my blood pressure is going to spike because I had a glass of wine or forgot my vitamins. There’s no space for that kind of heaviness in a group chat, you know? 

I feel like I’ve aged twenty years in nine months. I have all this "motherhood" baggage that none of my peers understand, and I can’t talk to them about it because it just kills the vibe. So I just… stay here. I hold the baby, I watch the fog, and I realize I’m grieving the version of myself that didn't know how fragile life could be.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I really thought those appointments would be a chance to exhale. I expected someone to sit down with me, look me in the eye, and actually talk through what happened in that delivery room. I wanted someone to say, "That was terrifying, you went through hell, let’s make sure you’re okay—not just your blood pressure, but *you*." I expected a space where I could be human.

In practice? It was just another transaction. The check-ups felt like a conveyor belt. They’d cuff my arm, check the numbers, maybe do a quick physical exam, and that was it. It was all about the "data." If my numbers were in range, they acted like I was "recovered." It was like they were checking the structural integrity of a building that had just collapsed, ignoring the fact that there was fire still burning inside.

Every time I tried to bring up the anxiety—the way I feel like I’m losing my mind or the way I panic when I hear a siren—they’d just nod and give me a referral to a counselor I couldn’t afford or didn't have time to see. It felt like they were pushing me out the door the second my vitals were stable. I left those offices feeling more alone and more "managed" than when I walked in. I needed a person; I got a chart.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

Yeah, there were. Multiple times, actually. I tried to tell my doctor that the panic—that feeling of being completely unmoored—was getting worse, not better, as the weeks went on. I told them that I’d have these vivid flashbacks where I’d hear the machines beeping and feel that cold, dark rush of the seizure coming on again. I wasn't just "sad"; I was terrified to be alone, terrified that I was going to lose consciousness while holding my baby.

Every single time, the response was the same: "That's normal, your hormones are still leveling out." Or they’d say, "You're doing great, look at how well the baby is thriving." It was like they were only looking at the baby, as if I were just a carrier who had fulfilled her function. They treated my mental health like it was a side effect that would just evaporate if I ignored it hard enough. 

It made me feel like my pain wasn't "real" enough to merit their time. Because I hadn't had another seizure, they acted like I was fine. It’s infuriating. They were obsessed with my blood pressure, but they didn't care at all that my heart was breaking every time I had to navigate this post-traumatic fog. They just didn't see me.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I haven't gone back yet, and honestly, the thought of it is paralyzing. Before, I was the girl who had her life mapped out—I was building a career, working full-time, and feeling capable. I expected that even after the baby, I’d just slide back into that rhythm eventually. Maybe I’d be tired, but I thought I’d be *me*. 

Now, the idea of sitting at a desk feels like a cruel joke. How am I supposed to focus on spreadsheets when I’m still physically vibrating with the aftershocks of almost dying? I look at my job and I just see another place where I’ll have to mask all of this. If I walk into the office and tell people I’m having a panic attack because the breakroom lights remind me of the delivery room, they won’t know how to handle it. They’ll just see the "high-risk" girl who couldn't keep it together.

It feels like my career is a place I don't belong anymore because I don't recognize the person I was before the seizure. That version of Kelly was confident. This version? I’m just trying to make it to dinner time without checking my pulse. I don't know how to be a professional when I’m still barely a survivor. It feels like I’ve lost my footing entirely.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

I keep picturing it, and it feels like a mountain I can’t climb. The hardest part is that everything requires me to be "on." To manage childcare, I have to be organized, on time, and completely together. But my internal battery is just... depleted. 

Even thinking about pumping at work—the logistics of it—feels like it’s going to be a disaster. I worry about the privacy, about the time, but mostly, I worry about the mental space it takes up. If I’m sitting in a closet or a breakroom trying to feed my baby, I’m not going to be thinking about spreadsheets. I’m going to be thinking about how my body failed me, about the meds, and about whether I’m safe. 

My health is still so precarious. If I have a bad night with the baby, or a day where the anxiety won't quit, how do I go into an office and work a full day? People expect you to just "bounce back" after maternity leave, like you’re just coming off a vacation. But I’m not coming off a break. I’m coming off a fight for my life. The idea of balancing that with a commute, daycare drop-offs, and a boss who expects the same performance as before? It makes my chest tighten just thinking about it. I’m scared that as soon as I try to step back into that life, I’ll just shatter.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I’m not married, and honestly, that makes the isolation hit a lot harder. I don't have a partner here to hand the baby to when the panic sets in, or someone to look at the monitor while I try to sleep for an hour. I think I expected that I’d be able to do this—that I could be the "independent mom"—because I’ve always been independent. I thought I could handle the shifts, the feeding, and the recovery on my own strength.

But the reality is that the weight of it is just… immense. When you’re doing this solo and you’re also recovering from something as heavy as eclampsia, there’s no one to witness what you’re going through. There’s no one to tell me, "Hey, you look like you’re slipping, let me take the baby so you can breathe." It’s just me and my daughter in this house, and when I have those moments where I feel like I’m being pulled back into the delivery room, I have to be the one to talk myself down. I have to be the one to reassure myself that I’m still here. It’s a level of loneliness that I don't think I ever really understood until I was holding her at 3:00 AM, my heart racing, with no one to hold me back.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

I think the biggest surprise—or maybe it's more of a realization—is how much of the "invisible" work I’m doing completely solo. Because I’m a single mother, I didn't have a partner to share that load with, but I think I expected my wider circle to step in more. I thought, *“Surely, when people see how sick I was, they’ll rally.”* 

But the surprise was how quickly the initial "congratulations" faded into the background. People check on the baby, they ask how she’s growing, but the "caregiver" part of the equation? That feels like it's entirely on my shoulders. I expected to feel more supported, not just by a partner but by my village. Instead, I’m the one managing the doctor’s appointments, the follow-ups, the anxiety, and the day-to-day survival of both of us. 

It’s been a harsh lesson in just how invisible the postpartum experience is, especially when you’re "high-risk." Everyone is so focused on the clinical outcome—did the baby make it? is the baby gaining weight?—that they miss the fact that the person holding the baby is barely holding it together. I’m doing all of this while trying to convince myself I’m not still broken. It’s heavy. It’s just really, really heavy.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I think, deep down, I expected maternity care to be a bridge. I expected a team that would guide me from being a young woman to a mother, especially knowing my history. I wanted someone to hold space for the fear I carried, to help me navigate the genetics and the risks so that I didn't feel like I was walking a tightrope over an abyss. I expected a level of care that saw me as a whole person, not just a set of vitals to monitor.

The most significant gap, looking back, is that I was treated like a medical case to be solved rather than a human being experiencing a life-altering trauma. They managed the pre-eclampsia, they managed the birth, they managed the seizure—but they left me to manage the aftermath alone. 

It’s like they were the architects of the emergency but refused to help with the cleanup. I was left in this quiet town in the Berkshires with nothing but my medical charts and a sense of haunting that follows me into every room. I received clinical survival, but I didn't receive *care*. I wanted to be seen and comforted, and instead, I was processed. That gap—between being "alive" and actually feeling safe enough to live—is the hardest part of it all.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I would take the focus off the "clinical end-point" and shift it entirely toward the humanity of the person sitting in that room. Right now, the system is designed to measure, test, and act—everything is about avoiding a catastrophe, and I get that. But once the catastrophe—or even just the birth—is over, the system acts like it’s done its job. It drops you.

If I could change one thing, I’d mandate a "transition of care" that isn't just about blood pressure or physical healing. We need a system that recognizes that the psychological fallout of a high-risk pregnancy is just as dangerous as the clinical risks. There should be a dedicated advocate—someone who isn't just rushing to the next appointment—who stays with you for those first few months. Someone who can help you bridge the gap between that clinical intensity and being a new mother at home. 

We need to stop treating maternity care like it’s a factory where you check out once the product—the baby—is safe. It’s a lifelong transition, and the way the system is built now, it feels like it’s designed to leave you isolated and traumatized the second you walk out those hospital doors. I want a system that validates that you’ve survived something hard, and stays to help you find your footing again.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only other thing I’d really want to say is that there’s this crushing weight of silence that follows you home. Everyone thinks that because you survived, and because you have your baby, that the chapter is closed. But for me, the silence is where the fear actually lives. When you’re in the hospital, the constant checking—the vitals, the meds—it's intrusive, sure, but it also means there are eyes on you. You aren't "alone" with the disaster. 

Once you get home, the medical system vanishes, and you’re just left sitting in that silence with the memories of what happened. I’m scared that people—like the researchers or the doctors—only care about the *outcomes*. But I wish they could see that a "healthy" outcome for the baby shouldn't be the only thing that counts as a success. A success should also be a mother who isn't shaking when she hears a siren, or one who doesn't feel like a ticking time bomb. 

If there’s one thing I hope you take away, it’s that we are not just our charts. We are terrified, we are exhausted, and we are mourning the motherhood we thought we were going to have. Please, just don’t stop looking after us once the baby arrives. That’s when the real work of surviving actually starts.
